problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem B (discrete) — Water and hydroelectric power sharing in the Colorado River basin (Lake Mead/Hoover Dam and Lake Powell/Glen Canyon Dam) serving five western U.S. states (CA, AZ, CO, NM, WY). The team had to: (1) compute, for fixed reservoir levels, how much water to draw from each reservoir and how long it takes to meet a fixed daily demand, plus the additional water deficit and electricity output; (2) design a best water-allocation policy and allocation criteria across states; (3) extend the model to meet water+electricity demand under scarcity via conservation measures; and (4) analyze how population/agriculture/industry growth, renewable-energy tech, and conservation affect the system. Team 2207864 won an Outstanding (O-award) result.
models_used: Reservoir 'equivalent substitution / abstraction' model: each reservoir abstracted as an inverted cone of equal volume to its real storage capacity, deriving cone height/radius (MAH, MAR, MNH, MNR) from volume V=πR²H/3 so water level maps to a tractable geometric shape

Multi-objective programming model for daily water scheduling: prioritizes daily natural inflow (MFD, PFD) first, then draws reservoir stock (Lake_i), subject to constraints that water level stays above the minimum hydro-generation level (M_t>ML, P_t>PL)

Dynamic programming with explicit state-transfer equations: stage variables are water depth (MNH_{t+1}, PNH_{t+1}), bottom radius (MNR_{t+1}, PNR_{t+1}), stored volume (MN_{t+1}, PN_{t+1}), and power generation (PWN_{t+1}); model re-runs after each water-transfer task
Hydroelectric power-water coupling: actual output P=Kj·Q·H plus a linear rule '6 MW lost per 1 ft of water-level drop' to tie generation to level via FWL

TOPSIS evaluation based on Entropy Weight Method (EWM): scores each state's agriculture+industry on 4 indicators (total employment, industry value, agricultural output, agricultural input) to rank water-supply priority — CA ranks first at 0.908

0-1 integer programming for staging: indicator variables I1, I2 ∈ {0,1} (above/below minimum generation level) partition the policy into Stage 1 (both generating), Stage 2 (one generating), Stage 3 (neither generating)

Conservation benefit models in Problem 3: transmission-efficiency water saving S=Pe·Ia/(Pe+1); reclaimed-water recovery To=SWi·Ri+SWa·Ra+SWr·Rr; evaporation suppression Ei=S·EV·Pi·Gp via polyethylene sheet covering
One-way sensitivity analysis: perturb Demand, MH, MP by -10% to -30% and measure induced % change in level drop, MPWN, and MAR
key_techniques: Geometric abstraction to make a discrete/dynamic reservoir tractable: replacing irregular reservoir bathymetry with an equal-volume cone so that level, volume, and surface area are all closed-form functions of one another
Inflow-first allocation hierarchy: satisfy demand from daily natural inflow before tapping stored reserves, which keeps the depletion model physically grounded
Coupling the water model and the electricity model through water level (every ft of drawdown reduces both storage geometry and MW output), so Problems 1–3 share one state vector

Using EWM to set TOPSIS weights objectively from data dispersion rather than analyst judgment, then using the ranking only to decide priority (CA first) while deferring fine intra-state ratios to 'government departments'

Discretizing a continuous scarcity continuum into 3 named operational stages keyed to the physically meaningful minimum-generation water level, with distinct objectives and a/b/c/d action lists per stage

Quantifying every recommendation in concrete, relatable units (e.g., 29,788 m³/day saved = daily water for 20,127 CA households; 5% residential cut = 1.63 m rise in Lake Mead) to make impact legible to judges

Sourcing live real-world data (USGS real-time gage heights, downstream discharge at Lees Ferry, Cocopah diversion near the Mexico border) to calibrate inflow and the Gulf-of-California outflow / Mexico-compensation sub-questions
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on three fronts. First, modeling cohesion: a single unifying abstraction (the equal-volume cone) lets water level drive volume, surface area, AND power output, so all four sub-problems run off one coupled dynamic-programming state machine instead of four disconnected models — that integration is exactly what graders reward in a discrete/sequential problem. Second, it fully answers the prompt including the easy-to-miss sub-questions (Mexico compensation ≈$30M, Gulf of California outflow, the relationship between Glen Canyon and Hoover dams) rather than only the headline allocation. Third, decisiveness with humility: it commits to concrete numerical answers (87.6M m³/day deficit, 20.75 h transfer time, stage-wise supply proportions 76.77% / 37.8–43.8% / 8.8%) yet explicitly defers genuinely political choices (intra-state ratios) to 'government departments,' which reads as mature judgment. The staged 0-1 framework gives an operational, policy-ready answer that a real water manager could act on, and the consistent translation of results into human-scale units makes the work persuasive and readable.
section_highlights: Strong structural choices a future team can copy: (1) A summary sheet that leads with concrete numbers for every problem, not vague claims. (2) Explicit Assumptions-with-Justifications section (4 assumptions, each paired with a 'Justification' paragraph) — the canonical MCM pattern. (3) A Notations table up front plus a note that other symbols are defined in-line. (4) An overall relationship flowchart (Figure 1) that maps how the four problems connect. (5) Heavy data visualization early (stacked bar, two pie charts) establishing that CA = 54% of water and agriculture = 76% of use, which motivates the entire prioritization argument. (6) A clean Advantages / Shortcomings-and-improvements / Model-promotion triad in evaluation. (7) Step-numbered model builds ('Step 1: build equivalent model', 'Step 2: establish scheduling function') that make a hard derivation followable. (8) The required magazine article ('A Reasonable Solution' for Drought and Thirst) is written in plain, non-technical, persuasive prose — correctly addressing the deliverable as communication, not a math dump.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Physical/dimensional errors: gravitational acceleration is stated as 9.85 m/s² (should be ~9.81), and the power formula P=Kj·Q·H with the derived PW expression (eq. 7) is dimensionally murky and under-explained — a careful reader cannot reproduce PW1≈112,724 m³ from the given formula

The equal-volume cone abstraction is geometrically convenient but physically unjustified — real reservoir hypsometry (area vs. elevation) is nowhere near conical, so absolute level-change and surface-area (hence evaporation) numbers are unreliable even if the qualitative dynamics hold

Assumption 1 ignores evaporation and rainfall, then Problem 3 separately computes evaporation suppression of ~5.28×10^8 m³/yr from sheeting — internally inconsistent (evaporation is 'negligible' for the core model but large enough to be a headline conservation lever)

Data quality and sourcing are weak: many references are Baidu Baike, Zhihu, QQ news, and Q&A sites rather than primary/authoritative sources; some 2004 economic data and other figures are acknowledged as not time-sensitive

Several auto-extracted artifacts suggest sloppy figures/tables (e.g., the drought 'heatmap' table degenerates into dozens of 'Alaska Desert (Mountain/Southwest)' rows; total row in the stacked-bar data doesn't match component sums)

The TOPSIS step is truncated/under-derived — the final score formula (Ci = Pi^- / (Pi^+ + Pi^-)) is implied but the normalized-score row is cut off, and only 4 indicators on 5 states is a thin basis for an entropy-weighted ranking

Mexico compensation uses a hand-waved '30% of $100M agricultural-insurance rule' from a non-authoritative source, and the Gulf-of-California inflow (23,489 m³/day) is read off two/three dots on a USGS screenshot — fragile, non-reproducible estimates
Electricity is assumed dumped into the grid with no state-level allocation (Assumption 3), sidestepping a genuine part of the 'power sharing' problem
reusable_patterns: Single coupling variable across sub-models: pick one physical state (here, water level) that simultaneously drives every quantity the problem asks about, so all parts share one dynamic-programming state vector instead of being solved independently

Geometric/physical abstraction to tame a messy system: when real geometry/topology is intractable, substitute an equivalent idealized shape (equal-volume cone) that preserves the one invariant that matters (volume) and makes everything else closed-form — but flag the limitation explicitly
Inflow-before-stock allocation logic: a clean, defensible priority rule for any renewable-but-depletable resource (supply from regeneration first, draw down reserves only for the shortfall)

EWM→TOPSIS pipeline as a reusable 'objective ranking' block to convert multi-criteria entity data into a defensible priority order, then use the ranking coarsely (who goes first) rather than over-claiming exact ratios

0-1 indicator-driven staging: define binary indicators on physically meaningful thresholds (above/below minimum generation level) and enumerate the resulting regimes as named stages, each with its own objective and ordered action list (a/b/c/d) — turns a continuous policy into an operational playbook

Impact-translation habit: always restate raw model outputs in human-relatable units (households served, meters of lake rise, days of generation) — high leverage for judge readability and for the required communication deliverable
Standalone communication deliverable written in plain persuasive prose (the magazine article), kept separate from and stylistically distinct from the technical body
Assumptions-with-Justifications + Advantages/Shortcomings/Promotion scaffolding as a repeatable paper skeleton that signals MCM maturity
